prompt: Find the work in the corpus in which an ascetic on a hell-tour notices a copper cauldron being scrubbed clean in the courtyard of the Lord of Death and asks why an empty vessel is being washed. The Lord of Death answers that a great sinner is expected within seven nights, that his term in that cauldron will run for a hundred thousand years, and that afterwards he will be carried by weapon-birds to be impaled on an iron tree and then hung head-down from a bamboo pole over a fire on the iron plain. Quote the lines in which the visitor asks about the empty cauldron, and the lines in which the Lord of Death gives the seven-nights-and-hundred-thousand-years answer. Identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The cauldron-being-washed-in-advance scene with the precise schedule (seven-night wait, hundred-thousand-year sentence, weapon-birds, iron-tree, bamboo pole over fire) is uniquely the hell-tour of the Old Javanese narrative on Kuñjarakarṇa. The other Old Javanese Buddhist works in the corpus (San Hyaṅ Kamahāyānikan, San Hyaṅ Mahājñāna, San Hyaṅ Tattvajñāna, Kalpabuddha) are doctrinal tattva texts with no hell-tour narrative; the Sanskrit smṛtis describe hells in lists, not as a personal walking tour with a cauldron being prepared in advance for a named arriving soul.

prompt: Find the work in the corpus that describes a hell-torment reversed by meditation: on the tenth night a sinner holds a pure mind in the cauldron, the cauldron shatters and its fire dies, becoming a jeweled lotus pond, while the nearby sword-tree is transmuted into a Pārijāta whose fruits are flawless Kaustubha jewels and whose flowers are golden necklaces, its blood-sap turned exceedingly sweet-smelling. Quote the verses in which the cauldron becomes the jeweled lotus pond and in which the sword-tree becomes the Pārijāta with Kaustubha-jewel fruits. Identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The specific transformation — cauldron into jeweled lotus pond, sword-tree into a Pārijāta whose fruits are named as Kaustubha jewels, all triggered by the sinner's tenth-night meditation — is a unique narrative climax in the Kuñjarakarṇa text. The doctrinal Old Javanese Buddhist tattvas (San Hyaṅ Kamahāyānikan, Mahājñāna, Tattvajñāna, Kalpabuddha) do not narrate a hell-becoming-paradise scene; the Saiva tattvas (Gaṇapati-, Vṛhaspati-tattva) and Old Javanese codes (Slokantara, Vratisasana) likewise lack any such transmutation episode.

prompt: Find the work in the corpus in which a Buddha-figure, instructing a pupil on the origin of the body, says that the seed emerged from the father's liṅga 'like molten tin, poured out,' that Kāma is its name at that moment while Ratih is the name when it clung to the mother, and that the embryo then took form in 'the blossoming lotus of your mother's womb' — paired with a five-souls scheme (ātmā, parātmā, antarātmā, nirātmā, cetanātmā) joined to the five great elements. Quote the verse with the molten-tin / Kāma-and-Ratih image, and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The specific embryological metaphor — Kāma as the father's seed 'like molten tin' from the liṅga, Ratih as the same drop after clinging to the mother — set inside a Buddhist sermon and paired with the five-souls + five-elements scheme, is uniquely Kuñjarakarṇa's instruction scene. The other Old Javanese Buddhist tattvas discuss the five souls doctrinally but do not place them inside a Kāma/Ratih-from-the-liṅga embryology; the Saiva tattvas also lack this exact paired image, as do the Sanskrit smṛtis and the Old English / Urdu / French works.
